title: chown error when the gid does not match user's primary group
body:
<!--
If you are reporting a new issue, make sure that we do not have any duplicates
already open. You can ensure this by searching the issue list for this
repository. If there is a duplicate, please close your issue and add a comment
to the existing issue instead.

If you suspect your issue is a bug, please edit your issue description to
include the BUG REPORT INFORMATION shown below. If you fail to provide this
information within 7 days, we cannot debug your issue and will close it. We
will, however, reopen it if you later provide the information.

---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST
-->

**Description**
When running `buildah bud` inside a Python process started using systemd with username `vagrant` and group `pulp` the following `chown` error is emitted:

```
error creating build container: error creating container: error creating read-write layer with ID "c4b29610e53808c294e23e6bb7d6fd682b8745b27d2cd745ad94e9cafe09fc1c": there might not be enough IDs available in the namespace (requested 0:65534 for /home/vagrant/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay/c4b29610e53808c294e23e6bb7d6fd682b8745b27d2cd745ad94e9cafe09fc1c): chown /home/vagrant/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay/c4b29610e53808c294e23e6bb7d6fd682b8745b27d2cd745ad94e9cafe09fc1c: invalid argument\nlevel=error msg="exit status 125"
```

Changing the ownership of `/home/vagrant/.local/share/containers/` to `vagrant:pulp` fixes the problem. 

Either the chown should use the user's primary gid or a better error message should be emitted. 
<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

**Output of `rpm -q buildah` or `apt list buildah`:**

```
buildah-1.15.1-1.fc32.x86_64
```

**Output of `buildah version`:**

```
Version:         1.15.1
Go Version:      go1.14.6
Image Spec:      1.0.1-dev
Runtime Spec:    1.0.2-dev
CNI Spec:        0.4.0
libcni Version:  
image Version:   5.5.1
Git Commit:      
Built:           Thu Jan  1 00:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:         linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman version` if reporting a `podman build` issue:**

```
(paste your output here)
```

**Output of `cat /etc/*release`:**

```
Fedora release 32 (Thirty Two)
```

**Output of `uname -a`:**

```
(paste your output here)
```

**Output of `cat /etc/containers/storage.conf`:**

```
(paste your output here)
```

comments.body: What was the ownership of /home/vagrant/.local/share/containers/  before you did the chown?

And if your user does
$ id
What is the default group?

Ok, but pulp(991) should not be mapped into your containers user namespace.
The only uids imappen into it should be 1000.

$ buildah unshare cat /proc/self/gid_map

Which means if you have a file that is owned by 190(systemd-journal),991(pulp) and group accessible to the user, it still will not be usable inside of the container.

crun has some ability to handle these GIDs, but I am not sure we have this in buildah yet.
@nalind @giuseppe should we have buildah "run" attributes to be set for the OCI Runtimes that do not get stored into the image?

Content owned by unmapped UIDs and GIDs is still readable if it's world-readable.  The kernel reports items owned by IDs that aren't mapped as having owner IDs with the values set in the `fs.overflowuid` and `fs.overflowgid` settings.  That's confusing when those values are ones that for which we've set up mappings, but the kernel knows the difference.

I thought the question was whether we should be defaulting to mapping the user's primary group as listed in /etc/passwd to GID 0 when setting up the namespace, or whether we should be trying to use the primary group that we're started with?
I think is explained, so I will close.  Reopen if more information is required.
